speaker: FL
words: salt. And then I'd open up them gates and that saddle horse I had and that old dog- I'd walk on up to open up the gates up the railroad track- and that old horse and that dog would bring all them cattle right up there. That horse, he was just like a dog. If get off the road he'd go right out after it and drive it right in. But that bull I was telling you about, that dog would just eat that bull up on the ground. By gosh, I've never seen anything- boy, that bull was scared of him! I know when Dad sold that bull he sold it to a guy in Moscow, and I went up with him to get him and I set that old dog on him and he run down to the waterhole, it was deep, oh, clear up on his sides and this guy said to me, "Boy, I'm glad you had your dog along." And I said, "Why?" Wfell," he said, "that old dog that I had, every time he'd get close enough he'd reach out and grab him." And this guy's dog run out there and about that time that dog I had bit him and he turned around and he said, "if it hadn't been for that he'd akilled my dog."

words: To get them back together?

But, you know, they'd try and try to buy some of them calves from him, he'd never sell one.

words: Yes, because in them days they could butcher. If somebody wanted a beef, a butcher shop, why, all we'd have to do is just bring one in you know if it was in good shape. And they sold a lot of 'em off and onto the camps. They sold to the camps, too.

speaker: FL
words: And you know, they got this good range up there and then they took 'em down on that range down below there, and I know ' --:- up here one spring, Dick Anderson run the meat shop down there in Deary, and he come up and butchered that. He said, "Boy, that's the only csw I've butchered that's too fat." When he slit that down the fat ran right down the end of his saw.

words: Well, you tell it,' I wasn't there. That was up at the McGary homestead, and of course, they lived down here then and he was real crippled, had rheumatism til he couldn'twell, he did walk up there but it'd take him hours and hours. And so she wanted me to go to the homestead with her and see how things were, and she had a little old horse and so we both rode the horse, of course, and one'd ride and then the other would walk, you know. So we got up there and I went in that cabin and here was bedding hanging up on wire across, you know, and there was a straw tick on the bed. And we got to looking around and there was mice in this little drawer, you know and we cleaned that out and went to bed that night and I could just feel them mice, thought I could, and so the i next morning went went down and picked strawberries, she had a few strawberries, put 'em in a gallon bucket. And we started home and we got down to the forks of the Potlatch and a a train was above us coming across above, you know, and she said I kicked the horse in the flanks, but I didn't and she got scared of the horse and she bucked and bucked Mrs. Mc Gary off and she was laying in the road and the gallon of strawberries was all over the road. Here I was sitting behind the saddle and the horse was kicking up, you know, and finally she stopped. And then we had to go clear back to that creek and wash '1 ' - strawberries before we come on home. She said, "Nona you done that." And I said, "Why, I didn't." I said, "I didn't touch that horse." She always said I did. Oh, we had a lot of fun. And they had some real old wine up there, and that evening she gave me a little, just a little wine. Oh, it was good, you know! I never drank wine, so I said, "Can I have some more of that?" "No," she said, "I think that's enough." I didn't know, you know, because it was old!

speaker: FL
words: Huckleberry wine. They mafce awful good wine.

You had to pull this little thing out to get the electricity, you know. And it would blister.

(Laughter) I sure got the whatfor! When she come down having them pigs in the house! Oh, gosh, they was cute little dickenses! I didn't see anything wrong in having them in the house!(chuckles) Do you remember that?

speaker: NWL
words: Nope, can't remember that. Now this McGary, he had what? Three. Jim and John and Florence. Three kids. Had the three children,

speaker: SS
words: You say it cost $25?

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, he sold 'em for $25.

speaker: LML
words: And you know, that was a lot of money in them days.

speaker: NWL
words: That was a grand machine in them days, too.

speaker: LML
words: Yes.

speaker: NWL
words: That's what everybody thought, and it probably wasn't worth any thing, you know. But, really people thought it helped 'em.

speaker: FL
words: I think that

speaker: NWL
words: the shocks

speaker: FL
words: But I didn't go much for this one. I don't know as it hurt you any.

speaker: NWL
words: I know we had lots of fun with it.

speaker: SS
words: Besides the shock, was there anything else to it?

speaker: FL
words: Well, yeah, there was a still current.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, that still current. Yeah. That's the one you put around your waist.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah. But that shock, that'd sure make the

speaker: SS
words: I believe it. You must have believed in it if you went and sold one to Lou Wells.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah, he asked me, "Do you think you could sell one?" "Some." And I said, "Well, I don't know." So one time we was going to Deary and I stopped in and showed it to him, you know, and so she bought one. Well, anybody could use 'em, you know, there was nothing to it. I don't remember what he gave me, he gave me a little something for it,

speaker: SS
words: Do you think the family would just buy it if they had somebody that had the rheumatism, or aches and pains? hifcL: I think it did help. I think it relieved it for the time being, but didn't never cure anything, I don't think. Well, I don't know.

speaker: FL
words: It never cured him.

speaker: NWL
words: No, he had rheumatism too bad.

speaker: LML
words: It seemed like his hips was 4roz or something.

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah.

speaker: LML
words: He couldn't bend his hips. He couldn't sit down. When he got in a chair, remember, he'd sit like that.

speaker: NWL
words: And he walked, you know, he had to walk with a stick. And he'd walk almost all the way up to the- not clear up on top of the hill- but he'd walk to the Jim McGary Meadow, and that's where they lived.

speaker: LML
words: It took him two days to get there.

speaker: NWL
words: It did, it took him a long time.

speaker: SS
words: — that kind of thing with the horses and-

speaker: NWL
words: You mean, us girls?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, when you were young.

speaker: NWL
words: We never worked out very much. There was no work around/yonly just to trade work is all. But we washed on the board and took care of just the household duties. But, boy, I scrubbed on the board many a time, about four hours.

speaker: LML
words: I never did.

speaker: NWL
words: No, but I did. But, oh, once in a while, you know, they'd need . to go out and help a little bit, and we'd go out and help our neigh bors, you know, if they had a new baby or maybe was sick or something we'd go wash and scrub for 'em and just do the ordinary things, you know, to help each other out. And when they got so they could pay, they'd pay us about two bits an hour. Wash on the board all morning and scrub the floor and have dinner and come home; two bits an hour! Glad to get it.

speaker: SS
words: Doesn't sound that much less than they were getting on the sleigh haul.

speaker: NWL
words: Right. They was only getting thirty and thirty-two and a half cents, Yeah, that was pretty good.

speaker: SS
words: Did your mother take a lot of time to teach you how to do things like cook and that sort of thing when you were young?

speaker: NWL
words: No, we just watched our mother and did it just the way she did it, you know.

speaker: LML
words: How come she never taught me to do nothing?

speaker: NWL
words: You was too busy outside riding horses.

speaker: LML
words: You're darn right! If there was a horse to ride, I didn't care if I cooked.

speaker: SS
words: They let you play as much as you wanted to too?

speaker: LML
words: Just about, I think did it all at home, Mama never let us chase around much. I rode horseback the biggest part of my playing days.

speaker: NWL
words: I've washed on the board for the neighbors for fifty cents an hourfor the four hours. That was pretty good pay for a girl fourteen.

speaker: SS
words: When you say, not getting too far from home; what do you mean? Did you want to go to Deary or Bovill?

speaker: LML
words: No, we never got that far away either.

speaker: SS
words: Really?

speaker: FL
words: No, we just entertained around in this country. No, she wasn't a person to let us kids run around.

speaker: LML
words: Pretty knew where we was at all time.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have brothers, too?

speaker: FL
words: One. And every time I done something I shouldn't why, he'd catch me every time and he'd tell on me. That little old cayuse, one time my dad said, "Now, if that horse runs off with you He was a fast little dickens. We used to go to the spring to get the water, so I just got on him with the halter that morning- then I wasn't afraid of horses, I'm scared to death of 'em now- and I just got on him and when we was coming home he felt good and away he come and he turned the corner up here so fasthe didn't make it, he fell down. Course, I looked up and there come my brother, and I knew I'd had it right then! He told on me. So I lost my horse.

speaker: SS
words: Did you tell on him, too?

speaker: CL
words: He was so much older than she.

speaker: LML
words: He was about ten years older than I was.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think she felt much different about letting the boys go, than the girls?

speaker: LML
words: I don't know whether she did or not, did she Nona? Did Lloyd just go and do as he pleased, or did she ride herd on him, too?

speaker: NWL
words: Well, not as much as she did us girls.

speaker: FL
words: No, he used to get around quite a lot.

speaker: LML
words: He was ten years older than I was and by the time I was old enough to remember, why, he was on his own almost, you know, then. And my older sister,.she used to slap me every time she went by me. Then when I got old enough

speaker: LML
words: I guess she didn't slap me as much as I thought she did, but it seemed to me that she was banging on me all time. (Chuckles) Maybe I had it coming, I don't know. Don't you say I did, either!

speaker: NWL
words: I was just hinking, I got a little place on my leg that you kicked me one time.

speaker: LML
words: Well, I had to defend myself some way. (Chuckles)

speaker: NWL
words: There is a little place right across there!

speaker: LML
words: Well, they picked on me and I had- that's the only way I could tect myself. I kicked!

speaker: SS
words: Did your folks expect you to toe the line?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes. They were strict with us. Very strict. If they tol4 us we could go someplace, we could go, and if they told us, "Now you come home before dinner or before they get their meal ready." We came home. They wasn't mean, but they just had discipline, you know. And they expected us to mind.

speaker: LML
words: We couldn't sponge off the neighbors, that was not the thing, we was not to do that.

speaker: NWL
words: No, they were a good father and mother. They was kind, but then they just wanted to raise good kids. And see how good we are! He don't think so, I've lived with him fifty-seven years.

speaker: SS
words: When you say sponge off the neighbors, you mean stay over and eat meals at their house?

speaker: NWL
words: I hardly ever did, I was too bashful to do that. Mom would say, "When they get their meal ready, you come home." About the time knew they'd get the meal ready I had to go home.

speaker: SS
words: One of the things we'd ask is whether there was much difference in the way the girls were raised and the way boys were raised, you know, in those days.

speaker: NWL
words: I don't think so. Do you? I think they'd expect those boys to mind and do the things that was right, just the same as the girls. I know my mother did Lloyd.

speaker: LML
words: They didn't want him to do wrong, I know that.

speaker: CL
words: The worst trouble with me, I had to grow up too darn fast.

speaker: SS
words: What do you mean?

speaker: CL
words: Well, before I was fourteen years old I was driving four up on the road grader. Before I was fourteen years old, I was thirteen years old, I'd been fourteen that fall and I was driving four horses on the road grader. See, I just had to grow up too fast. I had our team and Jim Hand's team and I was working for Jim Hand. That's what I say. So it didn't seem to mehatemuch time to play.

speaker: LML
words: You probably didn't.

speaker: CL
words: I didn't, see.

speaker: LML
words: Well, I went to school and the minute school was out a girlfriend of mine and I would go hunt our horses, they was turned out. And we'd walk about five miles, I think, to f'nd a horse to go get the cows that was about a mile away.

speaker: NWL
words: You didn't want to walk after 'em, huh?

speaker: CL
words: Just as quick as my oldest brother got big enough to go to work, he'd go out and work alright enough, but he'd never any money home, he'd always keep it for himself; never bring any money home, you see.

speaker: SS
words: And it fell to you to make some money.

speaker: CL
words: That's what I say. He was better than five years older than I was. See, out of 4 big family there was only three of us at home; my brother then a younger halfsister, that's all there was out of a big family, there was three of us at home.

speaker: SS
words: Did you bring all your money home?

speaker: CL
words: Oh, yeah, I brought every bit of it home. But that's what I say, that's all- well, outside of working for the neighbors- outside of working for the neighbors- I never chargedanything, if they wanted to pay me, if I took a team of horses and done something for them and they wanted to pay me , but I never charged them nothing. But when I was working for the county, I got two bits an hour, and a dollar a day for the team, then if I had four horses out there then I got two dollars a day.

speaker: SS
words: So you were working for the county then?

speaker: CL
words: County road district.

speaker: FL
words: I done a lot of that, too.

speaker: CL
words: Like I say, I started in when I wasn't quite fourteen years old.

speaker: FL
words: I worked in the woods when I was no more'n fourteen years old.

speaker: CL
words: I would have been fourteen, I wouldn't have been fourteen til October. I'd a been fourteen that October; I was thirteen years old.

speaker: LML
words: That's where your mother fell down, she should have made the older one help a little bit, too.

speaker: CL
words: Well, Clark he was out working, he went away from home and worked. Clark never worked around home. No, he never worked around home.

speaker: SS
words: From all that I hear, it seems like most all families were pretty poor and just struggling.

speaker: NWL
words: Most all of of 'em were, especially on these homesteads and that's why we moved up here. This livery stable, he put that in and figured that we could make a living, you know, that way. But it was a pret ty poor living, I'll tell you. My mother was a good manager, you know, and whenever we needed a pail of lard or something it was pret ty hard sometimes to rake up that money to get it.

speaker: SS
words: If you wanted a pail of what?

speaker: NWL
words: If we wanted a pail of lard or something- we'd get out, you know, out of something like that or a pound of coffee. And we never kept things ahead- couldn't like we do now, just couldn't do it. Well, it was tough times, but we never went hungry because she was a good manager, around the kitchen and home.

speaker: LML
words: We never had other things, but we always had all we wanted to eat.

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes. Just good substantial food. We never had nothing fancy, but we had potatoes and beans and garden stuff, after she got a lit tle piece where they could make a garden. There was stumps all around the house when we moved here, you know. And that's the way it was for most everybody. This townsite was full of stumps.

speaker: SS
words: Well, when you started the store, did that improve how much you had, or was that just about the same?

speaker: NWL
words: Well, it was about the same, only times began to pick up and my mother could bake bread and sold it, you know, and just little things like that to bring in a little more on the side. And it just kept building a little bit more.

speaker: LML
words: Them days I think they let 'em take the goods and then when it turned then they paid what they owed. Ain't that the way they done it?

speaker: NWL
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: You mean the credit til the crops were in?

speaker: NWL
words: There was no crops.

speaker: SS
words: That's right.

speaker: NWL
words: We'd have a little garden and my mother would can what she could. At first it wasn't very big, becausetoo doggone many stumps! But my mother was a good worker and she sewed. She was a seamstress and she'd do some sewing and get a little for that, you know; the ones that could afford to hire it done. So she'd just bring in a little here and a little there, you know.

speaker: FL
words: He was handicapped, too. He only had one leg and he couldn't get out and work like most people.

speaker: SS
words: Did he lose that around here?

speaker: NWL
words: Pardon?

speaker: SS
words: Had he lost his leg before?

speaker: NWL
words: No, he was in Troy. He had typhoid fever when we lived in Troybe fore we moved up here, and he lost his leg. Well, he lost his foot and then he wore an artificial leg. And in them days them wasn't very successfull either, you know. So he had quite a life.

speaker: LML
words: Well, he only had one eye, too.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, he could see. His eye was alright. He could see and he fell on a cornstalk he said one time and cut the bleeder that held his eye open, and made his lid drop down.

speaker: NWL
words: But we was a happy family. We had lots of fun.

speaker: LML
words: If that had happened now, they could have fixed it easy, you know.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah, that wouldn't a been nothing now.

speaker: NWL
words: And everybody was friendly, neighborly.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah, they all looked after one another. Do you think that had something to do with there being so little money around? That helped make people depend on each other more? Oh, yes, I think so. They knew if they needed help that the neigh bor would be right in there to do it, you know, to help 'em. And anybody got sick, you never went to a doctor from way up here un less they just had to. And they Would call on the ladies that knew how to give 'em aid, you know. And the babies was all born around here.

speaker: SS
words: Midwifes?

speaker: NWL
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Do you remember who did the midwifing around here?

speaker: NWL
words: Mama was one of 'em. And a Mrs. Grannis, lived across from us. They went a lot of times.

speaker: SS
words: Was that Earl Grannis's mother?

speaker: SS
words: Uh-huh. She was my mother's best friend, I think. They were just real close together because she just lived across the road there from us. Yes, they went whenever there was sickness, they'd call 'em to come. And they was just a little more talented that way, I guess than others. You could depend on 'em.

speaker: SS
words: When somebody was sick though in those days, was there really anything you could do for them besides sit with them. I mean, what would your mother have to do? Would she keep the house going during the time the person was sick?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, no, she'd just go and help out and then they'd get- or some other neighbor maybe would come in or they'd get some girl, that was home, to come in and help at the time. Well, she had her work at home; Mama couldn't stay away, you see, but she could go and help for an hour or two or whatever or whenever they needed it.

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, they delivered several babies around here. And this Mrs. Phelps that they talked about, you know, she was going to have another baby and so she came down here and lived in a little shack and she wanted my mother and- I don't know whether Mrs. Grannis or not- but my mother- there was two women anyway went. She delivered her own baby. Now that's something unusual.

speaker: SS
words: By the time they got there, you mean?

speaker: NWL
words: Thew ere there to help her, but she said, "No, I can take care of it." And she did. And that was that tall, skinny woman that Nellie was talking about.

speaker: SS
words: You mean they were there and she still delivered her own baby? Yes, she did. Course, they were right there to take care of it after wards, you know. But she said, "No, I can take care of it." And she did! Now, that's an incident you don't hear of very often less it has to be! But she did. What was his name? Where did I see that picture? Did we have it at home? Yeah, we had it at home.

speaker: SS
words: There was a picture in the . We-were looking at it not long ago, the husband. When I was here before. We saw it in the book. You pointed it out to me.

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, it was in here. Yeah. But he wasn't well, I don't know why; what was wrong but he was heavy, but he just wasn't well. So she had to take all the hard work. Did many people give up and move out? Leave in the years that you folks were growing up? Well some. Now like the bachelors that would have homesteads. Thy"d sell and move out, you know, or they'd go out and sell. Well, a lot of 'em did. And of course, Bovill began to build up after the rail road went through and then there was more jobs kept coming in so they could make a fair living. This is the one she delivered herself, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Was- like people around here, were they more attached to Deary than Bovill as a town?

speaker: FL
words: Well, I think this town was more attached to Deary.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, it wasn't quite as far to travel. And then of course, naturally the snow was out of the roads this way quicker than it was the other way. Roads was better down this way, and they was pretty bad too at times, wasn't they?

speaker: SS
words: Which times are you referring to?

speaker: NWL
words: Well, that was in the early days, you know, before the roads were gravelled or it wasn't even highway, was it?

speaker: EL
words: No. No.

speaker: NWL
words: It was, you know, there'd be just mudholes and you'd have to go through 'em and get through 'em the best way you could. We've got out of 'em many times, haven't we May? You'd start to Deary and get

speaker: LML
words: I guess so.

speaker: SS
words: Where did the road go over there, was it more or less wh ere it is now?

speaker: FL
words: No. No, right down by here. It went right down by here and turned the corner right here and then it went down through this way and the backside of Hal and then right on in there by the Anderson place and right into Deary there.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever walk there?

speaker: LML
words: Oh, yeah, we have. Not too often, once in a while, you know if we wanted to walk, we'd go

speaker: LML
words: Side D

speaker: SS
words: and sold it there at the little store.

speaker: NWL
words: You mean his folks?

speaker: SS
words: No, I was thinking of the general store.

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, he had just a general grocery store. You know, just like any littie store, he handled most everything like that. That - just staple goods because we didn't buy fancy canned goods like we do now, you know. But just corn and beans and just the staple stuff, and meat, of course. But he just had an ordinary store. And he had a few drygoods, not much. Like the lumberjacks coats and socks. But his dad had a bigger store. He carried quite a lot of the menswear.

speaker: SS
words: At Helmer or Janesville?

speaker: NWL
words: No, here, after he moved up here at the store. He didn't have any thing like that. Did you have clothing for men?

speaker: FL
words: Uh-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Did he just move his stock from down there up here?

speaker: FL
words: He moved it up here.

speaker: SS
words: He just took the store and moved it?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: NWL
words: They left that place where the store was and he built a new home up on the hill, and when they moved the new home up on the hill, why they put in a store up here. And he had a pretty fair store. Lot bigger than my dad's.

speaker: SS
words: When did you two meet?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, we met in about 1907.

speaker: FL
words: Went to school together.

speaker: NWL
words: Going to school. We had to walk to the old log schoolhouse, about a mile and a half up there. He'd walk on one side of the road and I'd walk on the other, and that's the way we met. We went to school to gether. Just liked each other, I guess.

speaker: SS
words: Had maybe even less, like when his father gave her food not really thinking he'd get any money back for it anymore. Was there much charity like that?

speaker: NWL
words: He didn't give it to 'em, he sold it to them. You see, he had to run his store on a basis, you know, so if he didn't get the money back in it was hard for him to get the food back in for these people. And he could let each one of 'em have so much credit, then he had to cut 'em off, you see, and that made it awful hard, too. And that's what happened to this lady when she came down, he'd just let so many different ones have credit you know. And so it made it hard on the people that had little store as well as the homesteaders but have to work around some way and get the money to pay these bills, and then they could go on, you know. But they'd carry 'em so long so they could live, and of course, when they could prove up on their places then they had money, you know, enough so that they could pay their expenses.

speaker: SS
words: Why did they have money just because they proved up?

speaker: NWL
words: What?

speaker: SS
words: Then they'd sell their timber?

speaker: NWL
words: Yes. They would probably just sell the whole thing, the whole home stead.

speaker: SS
words: Is that what most folks did, once they'd proved up on those home steads like around McGary Butte? Just sold them right away?

speaker: NWL
words: Not too long. They didn't hold 'em too long, did they? When they had got a chance to sell, I suppose, when the Potlatch come in. And .they'd sell 'em, you know, then they'd go other places or move into Bovill or somewhere in here maybe if there was a vacant house and then they'd go away back East.

speaker: SS
words: And go to work for the company?

speaker: NWL
words: Well, wherever they could get work, you know. And after the rail road come in of course, the jobs opened up, you know. Little log ging here and there.

speaker: SS
words: But with a lot of those other homesteads like on the Ridges, they just wanted to hold onto 'em and they didn't sell 'em once they'd proved up and they went to farming.

speaker: NWL
words: No, not all of 'em. Some of 'em sold though. And then of course, whoever would buy would improve the land and build 'em up, you know. And that's the way the farms got started down around on the Ridge. And it was good soil down there. And when one would buy and find it was good, then a neighbor would come from the East or somewhere and would buy. But most of 'em and a few of °em stayed but there was an awful lot of 'em moved out and sold. But of course, like I said, the people that come in would improve and plow more fields And of course, that was in team days, and then of course, after they got the tractors then they really went to town. But the Ridge is good soil.

speaker: SS
words: Your father, he carried people too on credit?

speaker: NWL
words: What he could, yes. But he didn't have the capital to carry- to be able to carry 'em too long. And then there was, you know, like all stores you lose some now and then. People that just didn't want to pay and didn't. But he didn't keep his store too long.

speaker: SS
words: Would people that wouldn't pay, would they still stay in the country or would they have to get out, if they didn't pay?

speaker: NWL
words: What—

speaker: SS
words: I mean, were you able to collect?

speaker: NWL
words: Well, no, there was no way of hardly to collect, because in them days they didn't have steady jobs. And some of 'em that didn't pay didn't work. They'd fish and hunt and live off of the others. We had one family here that everybody pretty near kept and he could 've worked, but they'd feel sorry for the family and everybody'd give 'em and vegetables.

speaker: SS
words: Did the husband drink or was he just lazy?

speaker: NWL
words: No. No, just didn't work. Just depended on the community to take care of 'em.

speaker: SS
words: But people did that? I mean they didn't kick 'em out?

speaker: NWL
words: .Ka few that would, but most of 'em was good.

speaker: SS
words: I mean, did the community let the family stay?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes. Well, they had a little place of their own to stay. I seems like they didn't rent much did they? Just build little shacks.

speaker: LML
words: Wasn't nothing to rent.

speaker: NWL
words: They'd build a little shack somewhere and live in it, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Did you feel sorry for the wife and kids? Is that it?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: SS
words: Maybe they had too much respect for-

speaker: NWL
words: Well, everybody had more respect for people, they thought more of their neighbors, I think. Now, there's so many you can't do it, and they're here and there and gone and there's ways of making a living now. And then there wasn't. It was hard.

speaker: FL
words: People that lived here was a lot closer than they are now.

speaker: NWL
words: It was just a different feeling. Everybody kind of looked after the other one.

speaker: SS
words: This family that you mentioned that didn't work at all-

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, he'd go out and buy cattle, maybe for a little while cr some thing like that. But he just never made enough to get along on and he'd maybe lay around for a month or two. They'd come and ask if they could get some food or if somebody had some eggs or potatoes they could borrow and then they'd go out and work a little while and they'd get a little grub in, you know. But he wasn't very well liked.

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, that's what I was thinking- I can't imagine he was—

speaker: NWL
words: But you'll find them in every community, in the homestead community or small towns, you know.

speaker: SS
words: I guess what I'm more interested in is the attitude that people took towards a family like that. They didn't drive 'em out of town on a rail or anything, they let 'em stay.

speaker: NWL
words: Why yes,.they had a right to stay if they wanted to. They're squat ting on a piece of ground somewhere and the family was nice; the children. And when you like a family that way, why, you're gonna help 'em.

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you too about how big an area would trade at Helmer? I mean, how far around here people would come in. You said all the way out to Park.

speaker: NWL
words: You mean in earlier days?

speaker: SS
words: Yes. In the early days.

speaker: NWL
words: How many would you say?

speaker: FL
words: I don't know.

speaker: SS
words: I mean, when you were growing up here. What did you say, Floyd, they'd come in from as far away as Park? Right? And from that way-

speaker: FL
words: There was quite a few come from the Ridge up there, out on that far ming country.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, yes, they'd have to come this way from Park or go to Kendrick wouldn't they, to do their shopping and trading. But they was just like we did; we laid in a year's supply when we could get it, you know, when we was like that. And that way we didn't have to go to town, you know, just maybe a couple of times a year, that is, for food.

speaker: SS
words: Where did you lay in your supply at? Where did you get your supply at for food? Did you go to Troy or Moscow?

speaker: NWL
words: You mean our folks, for the store? No. The rairoad was here then, and they could order from Spokane, and get their supplies from there, you know. After the railroad went in then it was easy to get the supplies for their stores.

speaker: FL
words: I can remember when I was a kid, he done all his freighting from Troy because there wasn't no railroad in here then. He used to take me along with him. Start out early in the morning and it'd be dark when we got back at night.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, after the railroad went in it sure helped everybody. They could get more jobs and then, you know, the stores were better. The two stores that was right here.

speaker: FL
words: Well, this country around here when they was logging it, there was quite a lot of work. Hired a lot of men to work in the woods. It was a good place to live.

speaker: SS
words: Did you and Floyd go together for very long before you got married?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, I imagine a couple of years, didn't we?

speaker: FL
words: I guess so. (Chuckles)

speaker: NWL
words: We kind of liked each other from the time we was small.

speaker: SS
words: from wbirt \foo u)fl kids!

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, no. My mother wouldn't let me go with the boys until I was, oh, I imagine fourteen. Then I didn't get to go only just to lit tle parties, just right here.

speaker: FL
words: I'll tell you something; they had a dance at the schoolhouse one night and their folks wouldn't let them dance, see, and so I and another boy we come to get the girls and her dad was sitting in the living room- I don't think he even spoke to us when we come in the house. He had the newspaper and he had it sitting there and he had it all spread out like that and this darn kid that was with me, he'd go over and read that paper and he'd run back to the chair again, and he just went through that-

speaker: NWL
words: Don't you think my dad didn't know it! He knew he was doing it.

speaker: FL
words: He knowed it alright but he was about half mad because we'd come af ter the girls, see. And so we went to the dance. You did take the girls? Oh, yes. You did! (Chuckles) Had quite an argument before we left. I'll bet all time he was grinning behind it. Because don't think he didn't know that Herb was doing that!

speaker: SS
words: That Herb was—

speaker: NWL
words: That was my sister's boyfriend that come after her.

speaker: SS
words: He was reading the paper?

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, he'd slip up l±kh that like he was reading the paper. If my dad'd a pulled that paper down about that time, I'll betcha he'd a-

speaker: SS
words: Why didn't he want the girls to go to the dance?

speaker: NWL
words: Didn't want us to dance.

speaker: SS
words: Was it religious?

speaker: NWL
words: Well, to a certain-- not fanatics, but they just didn't believe in dancing and playing cards and they didn't believe in drinking. My mother didn' t. And they were just a little strict. But it didn't hurt us any. It was good for us.

speaker: LML
words: Grandpa believed in drinking, though.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, he drank, yes, some. Not to much in the later years, he didn't,

speaker: SS
words: But he did let you go anyway? Even though they were sort of against it?

speaker: NWL
words: He didn't tell us we couldn't go. He just didn't want us to go. fwe was about sixteen, seventeen, wasn't it? That was over to one of the neighbor's house.

speaker: SS
words: When you were going together before you got married, what else could kids do for a good time, besides going to a dance? Was there any other places where you could go together?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, we did. They had literary and entertainments at the schoolhouse and Sunday School and then had little parties. And then we'd go out for drives, go to Deary. After they put in the theatre at Bovill, we'd go to the show there. Oh, we had entertainment.

speaker: SS
words: Did you go to the movies when you were dating?

speaker: NWL
words: Yep.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get married right around here?

speaker: NWL
words: We were married in Moscow.

speaker: SS
words: Did you get shivareed after you got married?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yes. Yes! (Laughter) (Nona is called to the phone)

speaker: FL
words: All the rest of the crew went I didn't I stayed to take care of the horses.

speaker: SS
words: Do you guys have much of an idea about these old-time lumberjacks- You worked with them. They were really different than-

speaker: FL
words: Now, they were the honest bunch of men that you ever met. They'd borrow money from you and they'd always pay it back. I worked at Clarkia and some of them old guys'd borrow as high as $600-

speaker: FL
words: (two people talking at the same time)

speaker: FL
words: — and by gosh, you had to watch 'em to keep the cats from running over 'em.

speaker: SS
words: You had to do what?

speaker: FL
words: Watch 'em to keep the caterpilars from running over 'em- the trac tors.

speaker: SS
words: ffhy is that?

speaker: FL
words: Well, they was just rumdum, when they come back from a spree. But they were honest old guys. I knot/ there was a guy they called him Sleigh Haul Brown

speaker: FL
words: Didn't hire no outside men, just picked their crew, see. And we'd come in with that speeder and I bet there'd be a hundred men flock to that speeder wanting to know when they could go back to work.

speaker: SS
words: Where would this be now?

speaker: FL
words: Well, you'd go to camp.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, at Camp 6, you mean?

speaker: FL
words: No, over on and then we'd come back in and them men would just flock around that speeder. And they'd want to know from us when they could go to work. And of course, we didn't know. And it got so I said, "Go to the office and if your name's on the book you're going out, and if it aint' on there why you ain't going out." There was an old guy that worked for the company I guess practically all his life and he rode in there with us and they wasn't supposed to even feed him but they didn't say nothing, and I took his lunch out for two weeks. He'd go out and stay in the timber all day and I'd give him his lunch and they finally put him back on.

speaker: SS
words: This was Sleigh Haul Brown?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: I heard he worked til he was real old.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah, he did.

speaker: SS
words: That's when they laid him off, eh?

speaker: FL
words: I don't know- they didn't lay him off because he was real old when he went in there.

speaker: SS
words: Where would the crews come from?

speaker: FL
words: Well, they come from all over the country. There was a lot of local guys and then they was a lot of regular lumberjacks that was out of the country, stayed around Spokane.

speaker: SS
words: That's where they come from.

speaker: FL
words: I know my son run a outfit down in Southern Idaho and I said to him, "Keith, I don't see a lumberjack in that bunch." He said, "They're about all farmers.". Old overalls and just shoes, you know. And one night when we was coming in from work and we went down he says, "Well, fellows," he said, "here's my dad, he's forgotten more about logging than I ever knew." And made me feel cheaper than the dickens!

speaker: CL
words: I know I got an uncle, I don't know what camp it was, but he talks about a long time ago when every one of them in there was from Fin land. They was all Finlanders.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah. I was in Elk River when they said they was going to build some spurs in there, and they got a whole- I don't know, there must have been fifty of them guys in there. One of 'em come in our bunkhouse and, by gosh, they'd come in there at night and you just as well get up on your bunk and lay down you couldn't tell what they was saying, all talking Finlander. And the guy that was swamping for me he said, "Floyd," he said, "let's run them damn Finns off tonight when they come in here." And I said, "Alright." And he was a big, husky guy. When they come in this Swede was in there and he said, "Now, listen, next time them friends of yours come in here you talk American or we're going to throw every dang one of you out." He meant it, too! They come in, but they talked American after that.

speaker: SS
words: I just wanted to ask you about your shivaree. Do you remember it still?

speaker: NWL
words: Yes, there was a bunch of the kids come down and they banged around; cowbells and tin cans and so we had 'em come in and then we had some cookies and coffee and stuff, you know, whatever they wanted to drink. They stayed a little while and left.

speaker: SS
words: Sounds like you got off easy.

speaker: NWL
words: Well, we did. Course,you always had to have a little treat of some kind, candy and cigars and things, you know for the ones that smoked. Oh, we had good times, oh, you know, the neighbors they'd have par ties once a week or once in two weeks. Somebody'd have a birthday and have a big party and get together and play games until it gotyou know, later years and then we went out. Used the cars and- just like they do now. We had quite a time. Everybody did.

speaker: FL
words: I think we had a better time than they do now.

speaker: NWL
words: We went over to the schoolhouse at Park one night to a dance. That's after we were married.

speaker: CL
words: That was in 1930. Yeah, that was the night that that thing went through the sky.

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah.

speaker: CL
words: That's when that went through the sky and I don't know where it went but it sure lightened up everything, I'll tell you that.

speaker: NWL
words: Was it a meteor?

speaker: CL
words: Yeah, I suppose.

speaker: SS
words: Went all the way to Park. Did you go, too?

speaker: CL
words: Yeah, I had my car.

speaker: SS
words: Just for the dance, huh?

speaker: CL
words: Yeah. They used to have lots of them and go to one house and .dance- That dance, that was in the schoolhouse over there.

speaker: SS
words: Well, did that change very much once your pavilion got going? Did most of the dances- I imagine they didn't have as many country dances as they did.

speaker: FL
words: I don't know. We didn't run that in the wintertime, see. But people used- I know, we put on a Fourth of July celebration and they had one at Lewiston, and boy, them businessmen was mad, it was hot and they all wanted to get out of town and they all come up here.

speaker: SS
words: I was going to ask you one thing about that bowery. Who built those boweries?

speaker: NWL
words: Didn't your dad build that? They just had a little celebration up there, altogether, neighbors, and they had a big time.

speaker: FL
words: Most every community that had a celebration would build one of them boweries.

speaker: NWL
words: Just a little place to dance and get together, you know.A It wasn't outside people at all, just the neighbors and just to have a little gettogether.

speaker: SS
words: How big would you say they were? I mean, how much room would there be inside of one?

speaker: FL
words: Oh,/imagine it'd be about- they varied in size-I suppose they figured on how big a crowd they'd have. The one I remember my dad built down there, I  it was about 30x40.

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, it wasn't too big, because there wasn't too many to come.

speaker: SS
words: Was it a rectangle, or was it sort of circular?

speaker: FL
words: No, it was square.

speaker: NWL
words: It was just a floor, you see, with poles up and a cover over it, you know, just for that time of the year. It wasn't built for winter or anything like that, but just for the little celebration they'd get together and have.

speaker: SS
words: They'd tear it down at the end of the summer?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: FL
words: Now, Martin Anderson he built one this side of Kennedy Ford and,by golly, it was going so darn good that he boarded his up and he had dances the wintertime.

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, he made a regular hall or pavilion out of it.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Just with the bows on top and everything?

speaker: CL
words: No, he put a roof on it, see. Yeah, his was going so good, see, he just started it there, you know, by golly, he just made a regular hall out of it.

speaker: SS
words: Was that the one that they had right by Kennedy Ford? Where it used to get flooded out by the river?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: It started out as a bowery?

speaker: CL
words: Yeah, that one that Martin Anderson had started out as a bowery, but didn't Haywire Hanson build one down there?

speaker: NWL
words: Who?

speaker: CL
words: Haywire Hanson; didn't he build one down there, kind of a round one like yours?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: NWL
words: I don't know.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah, he built his before we built ours.

speaker: CL
words: Yeah, that's what I say.

speaker: NWL
words: That one is still standing, that little old bowery on the top of the hill when we moved up here. The one your dad built.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Did they have one year after year?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, no, I don?t think so. If they decided to have one or built one, they'd build it for the summer, you know.

speaker: SS
words: Did people picnic in there?

speaker: NWL
words: Oh, yeah. Just get together, that's all.

speaker: SS
words: Did you ever go to revival around here? (Laughter)

speaker: NWL
words: Yeah, we went.

speaker: SS
words: What were they like when you went?

speaker: NWL
words: Well, I don't know. They wasn't too severe because, course, I don't know what you'd call it- Oh, yeah, the ministers used to come in here. They'd come in and hold meetings, you know, for maybe a week or some thing like that. But, not too often. But we had people here that could have Sunday School, you know. And they'd come from Deary, didn't they? A minister would come up every so often, not regular and go over to Park.

speaker: SS
words: Well, you know in the real early days they had a lot of camp meetings.

speaker: SS
words: I know, because they didn't have churches so much.

speaker: NWL
words: They never held them up here that I know of. They'd hold them in the schoolhouse, you know; revival maybe for a week. Some of 'em would join and backslide! And then the minister from Deary years ago would come up to hold services.

speaker: CL
words: I remember out there at Park one time, there was two of 'em, they was holding-

speaker: NWL
words: Huh?

speaker: CL
words: Oh, those two that come outto Park that time, they was there for a week. Come to find out they was nothing but German spies, but they come as preachers and holding a revival! (Chuckles) Yeah, they was German spies! The most fun that we had for a revival was down there

speaker: FL
words: remember down there. They go from place to place to get their meals, and Old Frank he was talking about 'em and he said, "By God, that was one time I kinda liked those guys to come around, at least I get something to eat!" (Laughter)

speaker: CL
words: But like I say, the most fun that we ever had at revival , and there was an old bachelor down ther,e fellow the name of Pete Franklin, and he was about three sheets in the wind-

speaker: SS
words: He was about what?

speaker: CL
words: About three sheets in the wind. And so after the revival was over then they was calling everybody up in front, so old Pete he got up there and got down on his knees and here come the minister to him and starts patting him on the head, and he says, "Have you got it?" Pete says, "You bet I got it I" And he pulled a pint out of his poc ket, and took a drink right there! (Laughter)

speaker: FL
words: Pete's liable to do anything.

speaker: CL
words: He says, the preacher says, "You got it?" And Pete says, "You bet I got it!" And pulled a pint right out of his hip pocket and took a drink right there! (More chuckles) Yep.

speaker: SS
words: Did you guys ever run into Dick Ferrell?

speaker: CL
words: Yes, you bet.

speaker: FL
words: All the time. All the time. I laughed at him one time, it wasn't funny either, but the way it all happened- you know he had that Aire dale dog.

speaker: SS
words: Dick did?

speaker: FL
words: Yeah. Well, he went outside and this here darned Airedale saw another dog and he took off after him and took Old Dick right out through the stumps and he was falling down any everything.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, I didn't know that he was blind. Dick Ferrell.

speaker: FL
words: Well, wasn't he, Carl?

speaker: CL
words: Not that I can remember.

speaker: FL
words: Well, I know when I was he come up there and he had that dog.

speaker: CL
words: You right sure he didn't have Fred Herrick with him? Fred Herrick was blind.

speaker: SS
words: Herrick?

speaker: CL
words: Yeahlnd Ferrell had him with him several times, and he had a dog with him; Fred Herrick had a dog with him, but I never did know of Dick Ferrell being blind, might have been Herrick instead of Dick.-- But Fred Herrick was with Dick.

speaker: SS
words: What was his preaching like?

speaker: CL
words: He was a very good preacher. You bet he was. You see, he was a pug to start with, you know, he was a boxer.

speaker: SS
words: How many of the men listened to him?

speaker: FL
words: There'd be a lot of em,

speaker: CL
words: Yeah, you'd be surprised.

speaker: FL
words: You'd be surprised.

speaker: CL
words: That cookshack'd be just pretty near full.

speaker: FL
words: You take most of those old guys, they wasn't religious, but they'd go listen to him.

speaker: SS
words: Was he a fire-and-brimstone type preacher?

speaker: CL
words: Not too bad.

speaker: FL
words: He wasn't too bad, no, he wasn't.

speaker: CL
words: No, he was a pretty preacher. I thought he was, anyway. You bet.

speaker: SS
words: Did you fellows know Malker Anderson?

speaker: CL
words: Well, I worked for him; we both worked for him.

speaker: SS
words: What did you think of Malker as a foreman?

speaker: CL
words: He was the best foreman that they ever had.

speaker: FL
words: That's right. He done it so much easier than the rest of 'em did-

speaker: CL
words: -there wasn't no comparison.

speaker: FL
words: No.

speaker: CL
words: noisy- He was running Camp 37 over there at Mason Meadows. I was up there at Clark's Landing— I didn't know about it—" Tom Kinney come into camp, and Malker was drunk and Nogle said, "I'm going to can him." And so Tom Kinney, he was there, he was the super over Nogle, see, and he says to Tom Kinney, he says, "Mr. Nogle, leave that man alone. If there's any canning to be done around here, I'll do it. That man can get more logs drunk than the rest of 'em can sober."(Chuckles) He just had a knack of laying out his work and everything just went so darn smooth.

speaker: CL
words: He had everything fixed up so you could work. When you went on a strip it was all skidded up and your land was fixed, you could start right in hauling logs as soon as you got there. But he was a good guy to work for, no doubt.

speaker: SS
words: I wonder what made him so good? He planned it better?

speaker: CL
words: Well, he was just a better logger, that's all. Just a natural born- His brother was a walker but he was so much better than Axel that there wasn't no comparison. You bet.

speaker: SS
words: Do you think he cared about the men more?

speaker: CL
words: Yes, you bet he cared about the men. You bet.

speaker: FL
words: He was telling me up at C'Ka, there, "They cut us down." And he said, "I went to the .men," and he said, "they're going to allow you to make so much a day." And,he said, "I'll give you the same money as you're getting for skidding as you're getting now," he said, "just go out and take it easy." He said, "When you get five dollars worth in, set down, don't do no more." And, he said, "You know, I hadAthem down to where they had to work just as hard as they could to make it." He said, "They wouldn't quit."

speaker: SS
words: He said- I had to - what?

speaker: FL
words: He had to lower the wages so they had to work real hard to make it.

speaker: CL
words: The price on the strip.

speaker: FL
words: He was getting them.cheap enough that they wouldn't a cut at all if they'd just used their heads. I know I was working for him and he put me on a strip and Old Buckley had it, and he give him pretty good money, and he said, "I'll give you the same as I give Buck." And pretty good timber. "Now, Floyd," he says, "don't put in too much, you're just allowed so much and when you get tired just go on the hill and sit there." And it was pretty good timber and that loading crew come down there and loaded me out every night about. I could have made big money there, but I didn't do it.

speaker: SS
words: Would he have minded if you'd have put in more?

speaker: CL
words: Wouldn't have got any more money, they was just paying so much and that was it.

speaker: SS
words: Oh, I see. See, that was wage? Right?

speaker: CL
words: You bet. Wage. They had a scale set that a gyppo could make so much and when that was made, that was it.

speaker: SS
words: Would Axel have wanted you to keep working? After you'd made the scale?

speaker: FL
words: Oh, that Axel, he wasn't nothing. I put in a strip over there and I went back, and the guy, the swamper, he waited and got his check, and I didn't, I come on home and then I got mine when I went back. And he said to me, "Holy Jesus, Floyd," he said, "you made more money than I made last month." I said, "I earned it, too." But that didn't make no difference, he tried to get even with me the rest of the time I was over there.

speaker: SS
words: You mentioned that it was - you said it was Dean - he saw you working down there when you weren't getting no logs out, be cause it was such bad conditions.

speaker: FL
words: Well, they'd logged it the summer before and all we had was the backend of the strip, see, it was all long haul. And he didn't raise his ante a bit and I says, "if you want me to go down there," I says, "if you'll guarantee my wages, I'll go." "No," he said, "I won't do that." "Well, I ain't going then." And then when he figured out I was going to keep my word, he said, "Well, I'll guarantee you wages."

speaker: SS
words: Do you have any idea what happened with Malker? Why he killed himself?

speaker: CL
words: I don't have the least idea.

speaker: FL
words: He just drank so much that he just—

speaker: CL
words: I'll tell you what happened; See my brother quit- my brother was running a camp and he put. Malker up there. Well, they was in the process of moving down to Camp 39. Well, Malker come into Clarkia and he hid his pickup and they couldn't find the darn pickup. And so Parker was super so I guess that when Parker got hold of him, Parker took the pickup away from him and—€Annd a the foreman. But then when he sobered up, I and a fellow the name of Albert Johnson, he was up there he was in the process of moving up there to build a railroad grade into camp on Oregon Creek, well Malker, he come along and was talking to us, "Well," he says, "I'm going to see you boys in the morning." And I says, "Are you?" "Yeah," he says, "I'm going to be../,- facade boss." We was sitting there talking and Albert and I was eating lunch, Well, that's what we was doing. We was set ting a toilet there in this area, there wasn't an office building and we was making a bunkhouse. We was in the process of/setting a toilet there, just putting a toilet in. And when we got done we just went down and set out there in the sunny- well, it was the first of October, and it was a nice day and we was setting out there in front, we was waiting for lunchtime, too. And Malker got up and says, "Well, I'll see you boys in the morning." He says,"I'm going to be Mallory's grade boss." And, by God, you know, he shot himself that night. And when I went up there, he was dead. But, I'll tell you, when Malker went on a drunk, that was it.

speaker: FL
words: Yeah, he just-

speaker: CL
words: He stayed there til he got over it.

speaker: SS
words: Did that take a long time?

speaker: CL
words: Well, he was about a week in Elk River, I mean in Clarkia, he was about a week in Clarkia. And in the meantime they'd hired another man to take Camp 39. He done the same thing in 1940— '34, when they had that big fire over there in there in the He was running the transient camp up here for the Forest Service. He had two assistants and the clerk, and that fire got so big that they sent Malker over there and Malker he was over the whole darn works. He was over the whole works. And, by golly, when he was over there, he took that Forest Service pickup and pulled into Kooskia and hid the dang thing and went on a great, big drunk. Well, of course, that ended him with the Forest Service.

speaker: SS
words: That was right during the fire?

speaker: CL
words: Yeah. You bet. They put . over that whole business over there; he must have had- he was over au least 5,000 men. He was over 5,000 men.

speaker: FL
words: : That wouldn't have excited him any.

speaker: CL
words: No, you bet not. But like I say, when it was all over into Kooskia he went and hid his pickup.

speaker: SS
words: When the fire was over?

speaker: CL
words: Yeah, when the fire was over, you bet.

speaker: SS
words: So, he didn't quit til the job was done?

speaker: CL
words: No, he'd never go on a drunk til the job was done. Then you wanted to watch out!.

speaker: FL
words: He did in camp, Carl.

speaker: CL
words: What?

speaker: FL
words: He did in camp.

speaker: CL
words: Oh, yeah, but what I mean- in camp.

speaker: FL
words: You never seen him. I know Nogle was over there two or three times and seen him. And said, "Where's Malker?" And I says, "I don't know." I did know but-

speaker: CL
words: I know, when he was in camp— When they moved Camp 40, he hid his pickup that time in the same way. Yeah, he was drunk over there at Camp 37, I know that. That's when Nogle wanted to can him that time.

speaker: SS
words: But this was something big.

speaker: CL
words: Yeah, you bet.

speaker: SS
words: Did he get mean when he got drunk?

speaker: CL
words: No. In fact, you couldn't hardly tell it on him.

speaker: SS
words: That he was drunk?

speaker: CL
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: When you talked to him that day before he shot himself, did you think he was feeling real low?

speaker: CL
words: No, he wasn't. No, because that's the last thing he said to Albert Johnson and I, he says, "Well, I'll see you boys in the morning." He says, "I'm going to be Mallory's grade boss."

speaker: SS
words: Was he drinking that night before he killed himself?

speaker: CL
words: I don't have no idea. See, I come home. But, he was a wonderful man.

speaker: SS
words: Did he have a big funeral? Do you know?

speaker: CL
words: I guess so. I didn't get a chance to go. Albert went and so conse quently Albert was a pallbearer and I wasn't. So I had to be there to gets the cats going in the morning to get water in 'em and one danged thing and another, so I didn't get a chance to go. Albert Johnson went; we was working together. And so I stayed and Albert was a pallbearer and I wasn't.

speaker: CL
words: END OF INTERVIEW
